title: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
display_title: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
wikitext:
[]: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally?             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Sat Jul 10, 2010 1:54 am (SPLIT from http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=9029 ) My g/f likes Eva a lot. She's at a lower intellectual level than me as well. I mean she's good with numbers, and I'm horrible. Lets just say if she has a 9-page government paper due, she makes me write it for her. I've also know very intelligent people who simply cannot get past the whiney-ness (watched the dub, of course-of course). Anno created Evangelion [characters/interactions] primarily from his own experiences and arbitrary emotions. People who can identify with these underlying themes are attracted to Eva. Also, some are captivated with [the second half] the psychological aspects and how it explores the human mind, an identifiable aspect indeed. Then you can go on to split hairs among those who like the action, who think Asuka/Rei are hot etc etc.... Some intelligent people who don't readily identify with Rejection, poor parental relationships, loneliness, misanthrope etc... can also dislike Eva. But you're right, the majority of Eva fans have more intellectual aspects when compared to, say, fans of the new Transformers that never heard of the Anime.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Jul 10, 2010 3:03 am ^ I definitely agree that there are so many things that factor into liking NGE that it's impossible to reduce it to simply being a matter of intelligence. But I think intelligence matters even if that's hardly the only thing that matters. Surely if you take two equally intelligent people and one connects to Anno's existentialist angst and themes of depression and whatnot and the other doesn't then their personal experiences are going to play a bigger role than their intelligence. But I think part of NGE's ability to move the audience requires a certain ability from viewers to "keep up" just enough to follow but not so much that they'll completely understanding what's happening. I chalk that up to what I might call intuitive intelligence more than intellectual intelligence; but I'm thinking the two might be related. You have to sense that there's more under the surface and want to uncover it, not just be confused and get pissed because you're confused which is more typical from people who have absolutely no knowledge about the themes being presented.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Mon Jul 12, 2010 9:19 am i think you guys are on to something here. although it would certainly be awesome to know the collective IQ of EVA fans as compared to all-around anime fans or just people in general, for me, it's definitely a combination of things, not so much outright intelligence. besides, how do you define intelligence? are we going solely by IQ? take me for example (um, i'm going to use my own experience here, not trying to be presumptuous or anything, but because i don't have many friends who like anime, and even less friends who know/like EVA-- i could count them with one hand and i'd still have four fingers left. erm...) : i graduated ninth in a class of 124 students in a school that's well-recognized in my country for not being easy. i went on to engineering school, graduated cum laude with a chemical engineering BS. last time i took an IQ test, i was "above average." i sat through general psychology and philosophy classes at school, i did well in them and enjoyed them very much. i'm no genius but i wouldn't consider myself unintelligent. but i could never delve into an existential debate about anno's motifs the way some of you guys do. i always feel completely out of my element in those threads. i think a lot of it has to do with the fact that science is a security blanket for me-- i'm much more comfortable with the sci-fi aspects of the series, even with the psychology of the characters to an extent, than i am with philosophical themes. i read the threads and follow them just fine, but i can't really participate. and to be honest, if the whole of the series had been like episodes 25/26, i probably wouldn't have bothered to watch. i think there's a lot of that when it comes to EVA's audience. yes, you have to be intelligent enough to actually be able to follow the plot, because it is very complex (i liked that intellectual vs. intuitive bit, jimbo-san-- although "intellectual intelligence" made me lol) . but just because you're smart doesn't mean you'll like it. i think to like EVA you have to have a certain mix of qualities: be smart enough to understand (or try to), really like certain areas of it, and be open-minded enough to learn to like the areas of it that didn't interest you to begin with. there's also other things to consider-- whether or not you identify with the themes, like eva 02 said. i think a lot of the people in this forum have been through some sort of depression, and can probably identify with the characters in the spiral down. i personally haven't, and i can tell you that watching the second half of the series was a lot like watching a train wreck in action to me... i loved it, because i thought the plot was amazing and groundbreaking, but i never really identified, and to be honest i hope i never do. out of the characters i found myself deeply identifying with Asuka, of course; if she hadn't been around (and if she hadn't been the way she was pre -breakdown) i may have been put off a lot earlier. also, simple escapism. a lot of people just don't want to watch things that are dark or have downer endings. one of my roommates in college, she graduated first in her class at school, graduated suma cum laude with a 4.0 GPA in the same chemical engineering class as i did, and overall she's one of the smartest people i know. when we were at school, i would rent random movies (oh netflix, how i miss you) and sometimes if i really liked them, i would ask her to watch them with me, trying to spread the love and such. once, we sat down to watch little miss sunshine . it's one of my favorite movies because it's just hilarious, and i think the characters are very real and relatable, overall i just find it adorable. when the movie was over, she turns to me and goes "why do you always make me watch these weird movies? that family was completely messed up!" it's not that she didn't get the underlying themes, she just didn't particularly want to see them in a movie. i can't say i disagree with that, actually; one of my own mantras is "i don't go to the movies to suffer, i go to the movies to be entertained." if i know a movie will end in tragedy, i'll most likely avoid it. i've never read an entire play shakespeare, not even the comedies. the notebook might be my favorite chick flick ever IMO-- if only you were to cut the last five-or-so minutes out. and yet, i still regard EVA as pretty much the best piece of fiction ever. i don't mind how dark it is, and i can see the positives aspects of the ending, because by that point i was so into the plot that i only saw it as interesting. anyway, rambling. just meant to say there's probably more than just plain IQ at work here...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Tue Jul 13, 2010 3:51 pm ^ You and I seem to have summed it up pretty well, not sure anyone could put it into better words unless you get some Sociologists to do some case studies involving Socioeconomic trends and childhood experiences in true Eva fans. Kinda makes you wonder what the trends are among Japanese fans, to wanna send death threats and all. Just out of curiosity - because I believe a good chunk of fans do - anyone have weak familial ties? As a child I had divorced parents, both poor. I grew up with my dad who worked & went to school when I wasn't at school, so from 3rd grade thru 6th I had to learn how to take care of myself, in a way. I had a list of chores I had to do before I could even reach the washing machine and when the broom was taller than me. I had to learn how to cook cuz my dad wouldn't get home till 12, which also meant I had to put myself to bed. Sure there were some days of the week it wasn't like this, but I remember having to grow up fast. Aside from my parents, my other family lives out of state and my mom's side is all but completely dead. I always had an unusual relationship with my father, not hate though - its hard to describe though things are much better now in my 20's. This sounds like Misato, and pretty much all the characters in Eva. Anyone else have strained relationship with their parents that may contribute to identifying with Eva? Mines subconscious I believe, I'm not exactly attracted to animes with parental probs, just the psychological explorations in eva.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Wed Jul 14, 2010 8:01 am perhaps there really should be a separate thread for this. anyway, not me. my parents are divorced, but we're still close. i live with my mom and my younger brother. my grandma is very much like a second mother, and we spend time with her regularly. same with my uncle's family... my cousins are practically like siblings. not so much on my father's side, but then again his side of the family doesn't live in the city. i think it's probably a cultural thing. latin american families tend to be more tight-knit than, say, families in the english-speaking cultures, at least in my experience. if it helps any, i've always known that my parents' divorce completely defined my view on interpersonal relationships and my necessity to be "strong" and not show weakness. that's a big part of the reason why i identify with Asuka, although the source of that behavior is completely different; a divorce is absolutely tame compared to her background, but i still feel i reacted to it much in the way she did to her own problems, albeit it was a less extreme reaction.

[]:             Quote Post by The Fourth Evangelion » Wed Jul 14, 2010 8:04 am Um, Yeah. I do actually... My father and mother divorced when i was young as well. I now have a stepfather, I consider more like my father then my biological one. The family of my biological father is cold-hearted, partiarchical hunk of a family. Their thoughts are mostly taking the negative side. They're just cold people, and I don't feel that much of a connection to them, with the exception of my grandma, who is the mater familias now. The family consists at least of three brothers and their children. all people born in this family are male. All females are from other families. My mother's family is MIA or something. I'm vaquely aware of the existence of some, and it might turn out that i get to meet my only cousin from my mother's side soon... My stepfather's family's a mixed bag. It consists of three brothers and a pater familias. Some people, like my stepcousins(three females and one male) and the brothers consider me family. However, the now divorced mother of my stepfather once said that she couldn't consider me family, since it wasn't of her "own"(!!!) so yeah... That and the fact that I'm an only child, makes me feel connected with NGE

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 14, 2010 8:19 pm Eva 02 wrote: anyone have weak familial ties?

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Wed Jul 14, 2010 9:04 pm It's the titties.

[]: Hilarity ensued.             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Wed Jul 14, 2010 9:16 pm Eva 02 wrote: Just out of curiosity - because I believe a good chunk of fans do - anyone have weak familial ties?

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 9:25 pm BrikHaus wrote: Rei's tits and pubes

[]:             Quote Post by planet news » Wed Jul 14, 2010 9:46 pm Usual Oedipus complex. I whispered many times to myself, "I am Shinji". Though, what most people, including myself, realize later is that they are actually Asuka. Also, don't insult your gf's intelligence. You are the one who ended up writing the paper.

[]:             Quote Post by Tails_night » Wed Jul 14, 2010 10:20 pm Well what cought me was a lot of the emotional aspects, when I started watchign it my heart was as closed off and secretive as terminal dogma. My expressions were about as numerous as Ayanami's were to begin with, I had some serious issues with my father, so I could relate, I lived away from my family for a great portion of my life. BUt the one aspect that I could understand the msot was "hedghogs dilemma" the struggle to get closer to other people, but at bthe cost of more pain. Then the characters started to win me over, firs Shinji, then Rei, then Kaji(the pervert who knew too much), and I found myself friends i never knew existed. I knew they weren't real people, but the fact that someone had the knowledge or the mindset or even considered the idea that people felt like that, kinda made me reallise I wasn't alone. Asuka's outgoingness despite being lonely inspired me to be a little more outgoing. You can say this series was a turningpiont in my life. Outside of that, I liked its religious references, I liked the giant biomech, I liked Eva purely because of everything in it's universe. It probably is my top favorite anime series to this day, triupmhing easily over ity blood, death note, code geas, and such.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Wed Jul 14, 2010 10:57 pm ran1 wrote: I WATCH IT FOR Misato'S TITS AND PUBES THANK YOU VERY MUCH

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:01 pm Okay, that's far enough. Topic, bitches.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Sat Jul 17, 2010 2:20 pm Sachi wrote: Okay, that's far enough. Topic, bitches.

[]:             Quote Post by Halicat » Sat Jul 17, 2010 4:36 pm On the issue of intelligence, I agree there is probably a certain degree of it that may be 'needed' to really enjoy Eva, more than likely, a desire to learn, think and understand, with an interest in psychology, religion and the human condition, I suppose. However, I think it's more emotional intelligence: our ability to empathise with others and understand ours/others feelings and their origins and the impact they can have, rather than say, having a high IQ, after all, human intelligence comes in many formats and Eva is all about the human heart and the pain it endures in life. You can find some of the most intelligent individuals are in fact complete fools with no real ability to relate or understand other people. As the familial relations, has Anno ever talked about his family life? It would be interesting to know if his family life had any impact on the show, and I reckon it probably did, if Shinji is supposed to represent him. For me, I'm very close to my mother, her and my father broke up with I was 14 [They were never married, so no mess with a divorce] My father is VERY stunted emotionally and never talks, my mum regrets not leaving him back when she was pregnant with me, he treated her like crap and wasn't exactly kind to me either the night my mum finally decided to leave him, it hit me pretty hard, but it hurt me more seeing what she went through after that since she had had an already bad marriage with my elder sisters' father who was a junkie and an alcoholic, it was a source of depression for me for most of my teenage years, and my mum still struggles a lot with her confidence and a drinking problem mostly related to illness, but we're very close. My sisters are completely different people from me, and if we weren't sisters, frankly we wouldn't get on at all so we're not that close. My dad? Well, it's not a great relationship but it's not terrible either I suppose, we still talk and keep in touch. I always just grew up feeling like I was a world apart from everyone around me, with the exception of my mother, my fiancé and one close friend, I've never really felt close or connected to other people, so for ages I felt maybe there was something wrong with me, I know that isn't true now though. I reckon it was probably because I felt disassociated with most of my family members? I'm not really sure to be honest, more than likely that had a role, but all the other kids growing up were too 'stupid and boring' for my liking, as pretentious as that sounds so I was always a loner, though, I could make friends and have fun a good lot of times growing up too. I always seen myself as being like Shinji and Asuka, Shinji mostly because I never really liked myself and just wanted to shut myself away, but like Asuka in the sense I always just pushed my problems to the side and acted confident and outgoing, even bitchy and horrible just to keep people away from figuring out 'Hey, she's really fucked up!' And I always seen Kaworu or Rei as being people I 'wanted' to be, ideally, be like Kaworu: warm, smart, loving and care-free, and I wanted to be like Rei because in a fucked up kind of way I just wanted to be emotionally dead so all the shit I had to deal with growing up didn't bother me any more and so I'd stop feeling like a fool every time I 'pulled an Asuka' and got over-emotional at people or about things.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Sat Jul 17, 2010 4:47 pm Having actually read the thread now, I can totally remember what made me relate to Shinji. Granted, I have big time daddy issues, but mine are kind of the opposite. I couldn't honestly care less about getting praise, respect or affection from my father; I really just want him to leave me alone. It's my dad that wants affection and understanding from ME. Of course, I don't care how hard he works I'll never respect him if he's just going to come and be a drunken bitch, but that's neither here nor there. I have so much ambivalence towards my father, I'm not even going to go there. No, what made me relate to Shinji is that, during the age of 15 and 16, I was always forcing myself to do things I didn't want to and always wondered why I was doing it. Now, I'll have to go into some marvelous pointless personal stuff now, but ever since puberty started I wanted to be a jock. Mostly because jocks are the sex. I joined a team, didn't fit in, although I tried hard to conform and get better, I felt completely alien and out of place. Do I want to be respected by my peers? Do I want respect from myself? Depression was another thing linking me to Shinji, but honestly, I likened my failed attempts at athleticism to Shinji's ambivalence towards piloting the Eva. That's a fucking stupid reason, but there you go. That was just personal relation. I have many far more meaningful and intelligent reasons as to why Eva appeals to me as a work of art. That's just the only way you can squeeze the ability to relate from my black and twisted heart.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Sat Jul 17, 2010 5:44 pm planet news wrote: Also, don't insult your gf's intelligence. You are the one who ended up writing the paper.

[]:             Quote Post by Theos » Sat Jul 17, 2010 5:53 pm Eva 02 wrote: Just out of curiosity - because I believe a good chunk of fans do - anyone have weak familial ties?

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Sat Jul 17, 2010 6:07 pm Eva 02 wrote: She asked me to write it, saying "I'm not good at writing about government stuff." I happen to love writing, and Government, I'm also able to use that paper for other classes with a similar topic so who's complaining now?

She's at a lower intellectual level than me as well. I mean she's good with numbers, and I'm horrible. Lets just say if she has a 9-page government paper due, she makes me write it for her. I've also know very intelligent people who simply cannot get past the whiney-ness (watched the dub, of course-of course). Anno created Evangelion [characters/interactions] primarily from his own experiences and arbitrary emotions. People who can identify with these underlying themes are attracted to Eva. Also, some are captivated with [the second half] the psychological aspects and how it explores the human mind, an identifiable aspect indeed. Then you can go on to split hairs among those who like the action, who think Asuka/Rei are hot etc etc.... Some intelligent people who don't readily identify with Rejection, poor parental relationships, loneliness, misanthrope etc... can also dislike Eva. But you're right, the majority of Eva fans have more intellectual aspects when compared to, say, fans of the new Transformers that never heard of the Anime.

But I think intelligence matters even if that's hardly the only thing that matters. Surely if you take two equally intelligent people and one connects to Anno's existentialist angst and themes of depression and whatnot and the other doesn't then their personal experiences are going to play a bigger role than their intelligence. But I think part of NGE's ability to move the audience requires a certain ability from viewers to "keep up" just enough to follow but not so much that they'll completely understanding what's happening. I chalk that up to what I might call intuitive intelligence more than intellectual intelligence; but I'm thinking the two might be related. []:             Quote Post by carla » Mon Jul 12, 2010 9:19 am i think you guys are on to something here. although it would certainly be awesome to know the collective IQ of EVA fans as compared to all-around anime fans or just people in general, for me, it's definitely a combination of things, not so much outright intelligence. besides, how do you define intelligence? are we going solely by IQ? take me for example (um, i'm going to use my own experience here, not trying to be presumptuous or anything, but because i don't have many friends who like anime, and even less friends who know/like EVA-- i could count them with one hand and i'd still have four fingers left. erm...) : i graduated ninth in a class of 124 students in a school that's well-recognized in my country for not being easy. i went on to engineering school, graduated cum laude with a chemical engineering BS. last time i took an IQ test, i was "above average." i sat through general psychology and philosophy classes at school, i did well in them and enjoyed them very much. i'm no genius but i wouldn't consider myself unintelligent. but i could never delve into an existential debate about anno's motifs the way some of you guys do. i always feel completely out of my element in those threads. i think a lot of it has to do with the fact that science is a security blanket for me-- i'm much more comfortable with the sci-fi aspects of the series, even with the psychology of the characters to an extent, than i am with philosophical themes. i read the threads and follow them just fine, but i can't really participate. and to be honest, if the whole of the series had been like episodes 25/26, i probably wouldn't have bothered to watch. i think there's a lot of that when it comes to EVA's audience. yes, you have to be intelligent enough to actually be able to follow the plot, because it is very complex (i liked that intellectual vs. intuitive bit, jimbo-san-- although "intellectual intelligence" made me lol)

Article: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
VA's audience. yes, you have to be intelligent enough to actually be able to follow the plot, because it is very complex (i liked that intellectual vs. intuitive bit, jimbo-san-- although "intellectual intelligence" made me lol) . but just because you're smart doesn't mean you'll like it. i think to like EVA you have to have a certain mix of qualities: be smart enough to understand (or try to), really like certain areas of it, and be open-minded enough to learn to like the areas of it that didn't interest you to begin with. there's also other things to consider-- whether or not you identify with the themes, like eva 02 said. i think a lot of the people in this forum have been through some sort of depression, and can probably identify with the characters in the spiral down. i personally haven't, and i can tell you that watching the second half of the series was a lot like watching a train wreck in action to me... i loved it, because i thought the plot was amazing and groundbreaking, but i never really identified, and to be honest i hope i never do. out of the characters i found myself deeply identifying with Asuka, of course; if she hadn't been around (and if she hadn't been the way she was pre -breakdown) i may have been put off a lot earlier. also, simple escapism. a lot of people just don't want to watch things that are dark or have downer endings. one of my roommates in college, she graduated first in her class at school, graduated suma cum laude with a 4.0 GPA in the same chemical engineering class as i did, and overall she's one of the smartest people i know. when we were at school, i would rent random movies (oh netflix, how i miss you) and sometimes if i really liked them, i would ask her to watch them with me, trying to spread the love and such. once, we sat down to watch little miss sunshine . it's one of my favorite movies because it's just hilarious, and i think the characters are very real and relatable, overall i just find it adorable. when the movie was over, she turns to me and goes "why do you always make me watch these weird movies? that family was completely messed up!" it's not that she didn't get the underlying themes, she just didn't particularly want to see them in a movie. i can't say i

Article: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
 goes "why do you always make me watch these weird movies? that family was completely messed up!" it's not that she didn't get the underlying themes, she just didn't particularly want to see them in a movie. i can't say i disagree with that, actually; one of my own mantras is "i don't go to the movies to suffer, i go to the movies to be entertained." if i know a movie will end in tragedy, i'll most likely avoid it. i've never read an entire play shakespeare, not even the comedies. the notebook might be my favorite chick flick ever IMO-- if only you were to cut the last five-or-so minutes out. and yet, i still regard EVA as pretty much the best piece of fiction ever. i don't mind how dark it is, and i can see the positives aspects of the ending, because by that point i was so into the plot that i only saw it as interesting. anyway, rambling. just meant to say there's probably more than just plain IQ at work here...

Article: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 10:20 pm Well what cought me was a lot of the emotional aspects, when I started watchign it my heart was as closed off and secretive as terminal dogma. My expressions were about as numerous as Ayanami's were to begin with, I had some serious issues with my father, so I could relate, I lived away from my family for a great portion of my life. BUt the one aspect that I could understand the msot was "hedghogs dilemma" the struggle to get closer to other people, but at bthe cost of more pain. Then the characters started to win me over, firs Shinji, then Rei, then Kaji(the pervert who knew too much), and I found myself friends i never knew existed. I knew they weren't real people, but the fact that someone had the knowledge or the mindset or even considered the idea that people felt like that, kinda made me reallise I wasn't alone. Asuka's outgoingness despite being lonely inspired me to be a little more outgoing. You can say this series was a turningpiont in my life. Outside of that, I liked its religious references, I liked the giant biomech, I liked Eva purely because of everything in it's universe. It probably is my top favorite anime series to this day, triupmhing easily over ity blood, death note, code geas, and such.

[]:             Quote Post by Halicat » Sat Jul 17, 2010 4:36 pm On the issue of intelligence, I agree there is probably a certain degree of it that may be 'needed' to really enjoy Eva, more than likely, a desire to learn, think and understand, with an interest in psychology, religion and the human condition, I suppose. However,

Article: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
 agree there is probably a certain degree of it that may be 'needed' to really enjoy Eva, more than likely, a desire to learn, think and understand, with an interest in psychology, religion and the human condition, I suppose. However, I think it's more emotional intelligence: our ability to empathise with others and understand ours/others feelings and their origins and the impact they can have, rather than say, having a high IQ, after all, human intelligence comes in many formats and Eva is all about the human heart and the pain it endures in life. You can find some of the most intelligent individuals are in fact complete fools with no real ability to relate or understand other people. As the familial relations, has Anno ever talked about his family life? It would be interesting to know if his family life had any impact on the show, and I reckon it probably did, if Shinji is supposed to represent him. For me, I'm very close to my mother, her and my father broke up with I was 14 [They were never married, so no mess with a divorce] My father is VERY stunted emotionally and never talks, my mum regrets not leaving him back when she was pregnant with me, he treated her like crap and wasn't exactly kind to me either the night my mum finally decided to leave him, it hit me pretty hard, but it hurt me more seeing what she went through after that since she had had an already bad marriage with my elder sisters' father who was a junkie and an alcoholic, it was a source of depression for me for most of my teenage years, and my mum still struggles a lot with her confidence and a drinking problem mostly related to illness, but we're very close. My sisters are completely different people from me, and if we weren't sisters, frankly we wouldn't get on at all so we're not that close. My dad? Well, it's not a great relationship but it's not terrible either I suppose, we still talk and keep in touch. I always just grew up feeling like I was a world apart from everyone around me, with the exception of my mother, my fiancé and one close friend, I've never really felt close or connected to other people, so for ages I felt maybe there was something wrong with me, I know that isn't true now though. I reckon it was probably because I felt disassociated with most of my family members? I'm not really sure to be honest, more than likely that had a role,

Article: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
 there was something wrong with me, I know that isn't true now though. I reckon it was probably because I felt disassociated with most of my family members? I'm not really sure to be honest, more than likely that had a role, but all the other kids growing up were too 'stupid and boring' for my liking, as pretentious as that sounds so I was always a loner, though, I could make friends and have fun a good lot of times growing up too. I always seen myself as being like Shinji and Asuka, Shinji mostly because I never really liked myself and just wanted to shut myself away, but like Asuka in the sense I always just pushed my problems to the side and acted confident and outgoing, even bitchy and horrible just to keep people away from figuring out 'Hey, she's really fucked up!' And I always seen Kaworu or Rei as being people I 'wanted' to be, ideally, be like Kaworu: warm, smart, loving and care-free, and I wanted to be like Rei because in a fucked up kind of way I just wanted to be emotionally dead so all the shit I had to deal with growing up didn't bother me any more and so I'd stop feeling like a fool every time I 'pulled an Asuka' and got over-emotional at people or about things.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Sat Jul 17, 2010 4:47 pm Having actually read the thread now, I can totally remember what made me relate to Shinji. Granted, I have big time daddy issues, but mine are kind of the opposite. I couldn't honestly care less about getting praise, respect or affection from my father; I really just want him to leave me alone. It's my dad that wants affection and understanding from ME. Of course, I don't care how hard he works I'll never respect him if he's just going to come and be a drunken bitch, but that's neither here nor there. I have so much ambivalence towards my father, I'm not even going to go there. No, what made me relate to Shinji is that, during the age of 15 and 16, I was always forcing myself to do things I didn't want to and always wondered why I was doing it. Now, I'll have to go into some marvelous pointless personal stuff now, but ever since puberty started I wanted to be a jock. Mostly because

Article: What About the Audience Makes Eva Apealing Emotionally
 always forcing myself to do things I didn't want to and always wondered why I was doing it. Now, I'll have to go into some marvelous pointless personal stuff now, but ever since puberty started I wanted to be a jock. Mostly because jocks are the sex. I joined a team, didn't fit in, although I tried hard to conform and get better, I felt completely alien and out of place. Do I want to be respected by my peers? Do I want respect from myself? Depression was another thing linking me to Shinji, but honestly, I likened my failed attempts at athleticism to Shinji's ambivalence towards piloting the Eva. That's a fucking stupid reason, but there you go. That was just personal relation. I have many far more meaningful and intelligent reasons as to why Eva appeals to me as a work of art. That's just the only way you can squeeze the ability to relate from my black and twisted heart.
